description: Population-level health — epidemiology, prevention, health equity, social determinants. The discipline that sees health as a property of systems, not just bodies.

dome_relevance.4: Health — the core layer, population-level and individual
dome_relevance.8: Community — health as community property, not individual attribute

sphere_relevance.3: Environmental — contamination, air quality, water safety
sphere_relevance.9: Policy — health policy, building codes, food safety

adjacent_disciplines.description: Disease patterns, outbreak investigation, statistical modeling
Disproportionate environmental burden on marginalized communities
Ethics of health interventions, research, resource allocation

practitioners.name: Rudolf Virchow
practitioners.practice: Cellular pathology, social medicine, politics as medicine writ large
practitioners.approach: Medicine is a social science, and politics is nothing but medicine on a grand scale. Disease is not a natural event — it's a social product of conditions. If conditions produce typhus, fixing conditions fixes typhus. The physician is the natural attorney of the poor.
practitioners.body_of_work: Founded cellular pathology. Investigated typhus epidemic in Upper Silesia (1848) and concluded the cause was poverty, not microbes. Wrote that the prescription was democracy, education, freedom, and prosperity. Built Berlin's sewer system. Served in the Reichstag.
practitioners.why_instructive: He saw that health (dome layer 4) is downstream of every other layer — legal, economic, housing, education. The dome schema embodies his insight: you can't have health without fixing everything else.

dome_relevance.4: Health as social product, not biological accident
dome_relevance.5: Housing conditions as primary health determinant

sphere_relevance.9: Health policy as the most powerful policy lever

practitioners.name: Paul Farmer
practitioners.practice: Global health equity, treating the poor with the same standard as the rich, structural violence
practitioners.approach: The idea that poor people can only receive 'appropriate technology' is moral garbage. Give the best care to the poorest people. Structural violence — poverty, racism, political oppression — is the root cause of disease. Treatment without addressing structures is palliative at best.
practitioners.body_of_work: Co-founded Partners In Health. Treated MDR-TB in Haiti and Peru when the world said it was cost-ineffective. Built Butaro Hospital in Rwanda. Proved that world-class care in the poorest settings both works and is affordable. Mountains Beyond Mountains documents his work.
practitioners.why_instructive: He proved the dome's premise: coordinated, person-centered care in the hardest settings is both possible and cheaper than fragmented alternatives. His work IS the delta calculation.

dome_relevance.1: Rights-based approach — healthcare as human right
dome_relevance.3: Fiscal — proved comprehensive care costs less than fragmented
dome_relevance.4: Health — world-class care regardless of setting
dome_relevance.5: Housing — you can't treat TB if the patient has no roof
dome_relevance.8: Community health workers as the backbone
dome_relevance.12: Flourishing — his patients didn't just survive, they thrived

sphere_relevance.2: Built hospitals as infrastructure in places that had none
sphere_relevance.5: Community health model — accompaniment over intervention
sphere_relevance.9: Changed WHO policy on MDR-TB treatment

practitioners.name: Dr. Bernard Rieux
practitioners.creator: Albert Camus
practitioners.practice: Medicine during epidemic, documentation as witness, refusing to accept the unacceptable
practitioners.approach: There is no heroism in fighting plague. There is only doing what must be done. The doctor's role is not to cure — the plague will take who it takes — but to witness, to count, to not look away. Decency is not a grand gesture, it's showing up every day.
practitioners.body_of_work: Treats plague victims in Oran when the city is quarantined. Maintains meticulous records. Refuses to call his work heroic. Continues after his wife dies. Narrates the chronicle precisely because someone must bear witness.
practitioners.why_instructive: He embodies what the health layer requires: not heroism but persistence. The dome doesn't need heroes — it needs people who show up every day and count accurately. His witness IS the data layer.

dome_relevance.4: Health as daily practice, not dramatic intervention
dome_relevance.8: Community solidarity in crisis — the ordinary decency of showing up

sphere_relevance.5: Social fabric under extreme stress — who stays, who helps
sphere_relevance.6: Temporal — the plague as defining moment in a place's history

practitioners.name: Harriet A. Washington
practitioners.practice: Medical ethics, history of medical racism, environmental racism in health
practitioners.approach: Medical abuse of Black Americans isn't historical — it's structural and ongoing. From Tuskegee to toxic waste siting to algorithmic bias in care allocation. The health system itself is a social determinant of health for Black people.
practitioners.body_of_work: Medical Apartheid (2006) — comprehensive history of medical experimentation on Black Americans. A Terrible Thing to Waste (2019) — environmental racism's neurological damage. Carte Blanche (2021) — vaccine hesitancy's rational roots in medical abuse.
practitioners.why_instructive: The dome must reckon with the fact that its own systems — health, legal, housing — have been instruments of harm. Her work ensures the dome sees its own shadow.

dome_relevance.1: Legal history of medical experimentation without consent
dome_relevance.4: Health — medical racism as ongoing structural force
dome_relevance.8: Community — trust destruction and its consequences

sphere_relevance.3: Environmental racism — toxic facilities sited near Black communities
sphere_relevance.5: Social — racial demographics predict environmental exposure
sphere_relevance.9: Policy — how zoning and siting decisions encode racism

practitioners.name: Offred
practitioners.creator: Margaret Atwood
practitioners.practice: Reproductive coercion, bodily autonomy stripped, memory as resistance
practitioners.approach: The state controls the body. Health is not care — it is surveillance and extraction. The handmaid's body is public infrastructure. She resists through memory, through naming, through refusing to forget who she was.
practitioners.body_of_work: Offred navigates Gilead where women's bodies are state property for reproduction. Health monitoring is total but health care is nonexistent — she is maintained as a vessel. Her resistance is internal: she remembers her name, her daughter, her life before.
practitioners.why_instructive: She shows what happens when health (layer 4) serves the state instead of the person. The dome must always center the person's autonomy over the system's needs. Her body IS a dome that has been inverted.

dome_relevance.1: Legal rights abolished — the person becomes property
dome_relevance.4: Health as surveillance and reproductive extraction
dome_relevance.10: Autonomy destroyed and internally preserved through memory

sphere_relevance.9: Policy as totalitarian control — what happens when the policy layer serves power
